name: Technological Singularity
description: The Technological Singularity is a hypothetical point in time when technological growth becomes uncontrollable and irreversible, resulting in unforeseeable changes to human civilization. Most proponents believe this hinges on the creation of superintelligence – artificial intelligence significantly surpassing human cognitive abilities. This superintelligence, they argue, would be capable of recursively self-improving, leading to an intelligence explosion. This rapid and transformative development could fundamentally alter or end human existence as we know it. The concept originated in science fiction, notably with Stanislaw Lem's work, and was popularized by figures like Vernor Vinge and Ray Kurzweil. Proponents cite advancements in AI, computing power (Moore's Law), nanotechnology, and biotechnology as evidence that we are approaching this point. They argue that these technologies are converging and accelerating at an exponential rate. Mainstream scholarly perspectives are more cautious. While acknowledging the rapid pace of technological change, many scientists and philosophers question the inevitability and nature of the Singularity. They argue that predicting the future is inherently difficult, and that there are significant technical and ethical hurdles to overcome before superintelligence can be achieved. Criticisms include the difficulty of defining and measuring intelligence, the potential for AI to be aligned with human values, and the possibility that technological progress may slow down or plateau. Some argue that the Singularity is a form of technological utopianism or a secularized version of apocalyptic narratives.
subtitle: Uncontrollable technological growth and its consequences

claims.evidence: Rapid advancements in AI and computing power.
claims.criticism: Significant technical challenges remain in creating general AI and achieving human-level intelligence.

claims.claim: Superintelligence will recursively self-improve, leading to an intelligence explosion.
claims.criticism: It's uncertain whether AI will be able to improve itself without human intervention or guidance.

claims.claim: The Singularity will fundamentally alter or end human civilization.
claims.criticism: The impact of superintelligence is highly speculative and dependent on its alignment with human values.

keyFigures.name: Ray Kurzweil
keyFigures.role: Advocate and predictor of the Singularity.

keyFigures.name: Stuart Russell
keyFigures.role: AI researcher and critic, emphasizing AI safety.

mythologyConnections: Promethean myth of technology granting godlike powers

timeline.event: Alan Turing's 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence' paper.
Dartmouth Workshop, considered the birth of AI.
Vernor Vinge's 'The Coming Technological Singularity' essay.
Ray Kurzweil's 'The Singularity Is Near' published.

sources.author: Stuart Russell
sources.work: Human Compatible: Artificial Intelligence and the Problem of Control
sources.title: Stuart Russell
